era_context: The mid-1770s sat at the confluence of the European Enlightenment, escalating colonial resistance in British North America, and a global imperial order dominated by Britain, France, Spain, the Qing dynasty, and the Ottoman Empire. Enlightenment ideas about natural rights, popular sovereignty, and rational governance were being translated from philosophical treatises into political action. The Atlantic world was deeply interconnected through trade — including the slave trade at or near its peak volume — and rivalry between European empires shaped events from India to the Caribbean. In East Asia, the Qing dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor remained the world's most populous and economically productive state, while in Southern Africa, Southeastern Asia, and the Pacific, indigenous polities operated largely outside European documentary traditions.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading posts and the Ethiopian highlands
Interior Southeast Asian polities beyond court chronicles
Central Asian steppe communities between Qing and Russian imperial documentation

events.title: Battles of Lexington and Concord
events.description: On 19 April 1775, British regulars under Lieutenant Colonel Francis Smith marched from Boston to seize colonial militia arms stores at Concord. They were met by militia at Lexington Green, where shots were exchanged — the 'shot heard round the world' — and then faced stiff resistance at Concord's North Bridge. On their retreat to Boston, the regulars suffered heavy casualties from colonial militia firing from behind stone walls and trees. The engagements killed 73 British soldiers and 49 colonists, and marked the outbreak of open armed conflict in the American Revolution.
key_figures: Major John Pitcairn (British officer at Lexington)

sources.name: Lieutenant Colonel Smith's report to General Gage, 22 April 1775
Depositions of Lexington militiamen taken 25 April 1775 by the Massachusetts Provincial Congress
David Hackett Fischer, Paul Revere's Ride (1994)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by both British military records and colonial depositions taken within days. Multiple eyewitness accounts survive.

events.title: Battle of Bunker Hill (Breed's Hill)
events.description: On 17 June 1775, British forces under Major General William Howe assaulted colonial fortifications on Breed's Hill (adjacent to Bunker Hill) overlooking Boston harbour. The colonials repulsed two frontal assaults before running out of ammunition and retreating during the third. British casualties were staggering — roughly 1,054 of approximately 2,200 engaged, including 226 killed — making it the bloodiest single engagement of the entire war. The battle demonstrated that colonial militia could stand against British regulars and stiffened colonial resolve, though it also showed the limits of untrained forces against disciplined troops.
key_figures: Major General William Howe (British assault commander)
Colonel William Prescott (colonial commander on Breed's Hill)
Dr. Joseph Warren (colonial leader, killed in battle)

sources.name: General Gage's official dispatch to Lord Dartmouth, 25 June 1775
Richard Frothingham, History of the Siege of Boston (1849) — compiles extensive primary documentation
Nathaniel Philbrick, Bunker Hill: A City, a Siege, a Revolution (2013)

events.certainty_note: Thoroughly documented from both sides. British casualty returns and colonial after-action accounts survive.

events.title: Second Continental Congress convenes and assumes war powers
events.description: The Second Continental Congress convened on 10 May 1775 in Philadelphia, weeks after Lexington and Concord. Over the following months it effectively became a de facto national government for the rebelling colonies: it authorised the creation of a Continental Army on 14 June, appointed George Washington as commander-in-chief on 15 June, issued the Olive Branch Petition to King George III on 5 July as a final attempt at reconciliation, and began organising financial and diplomatic functions. The Congress operated without a constitutional basis or formal executive authority, improvising governance under wartime pressure.
key_figures: John Adams (Massachusetts delegate, advocate for military preparation)
John Dickinson (author of Olive Branch Petition)

sources.name: Journals of the Continental Congress, 1774–1789 (Library of Congress edition)
John Adams diary and letters, May–July 1775 (Adams Family Papers, Massachusetts Historical Society)
Jack N. Rakove, The Beginnings of National Politics: An Interpretive History of the Continental Congress (1979)

events.certainty_note: Full congressional journals survive. Extensive delegate correspondence corroborates the record.

events.title: American invasion of Canada (Quebec Campaign)
events.description: In the autumn of 1775, Congress authorised a two-pronged invasion of British-held Quebec, hoping to bring Canada into the rebellion as a fourteenth colony. Brigadier General Richard Montgomery advanced north via Lake Champlain and captured Montreal on 13 November, while Colonel Benedict Arnold led a gruelling march through the Maine wilderness to reach Quebec City. The combined assault on Quebec on 31 December failed disastrously: Montgomery was killed, Arnold was wounded, and roughly 400 Americans were captured. The campaign's failure meant Canada would remain British, fundamentally shaping North American geopolitics.
key_figures: Brigadier General Richard Montgomery (killed at Quebec)
Colonel Benedict Arnold (wounded leading assault column)
Guy Carleton (British Governor of Quebec, defended the province)
General Philip Schuyler (initial Northern Army commander, replaced due to illness)

sources.name: Arnold's journal of the march to Quebec, September–November 1775
Guy Carleton's dispatches to Lord Dartmouth, 1775
Mark R. Anderson, The Battle for the Fourteenth Colony: America's War of Liberation in Canada, 1774–1776 (2013)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented from both sides. Multiple participant journals and official correspondence survive.

events.title: Lord Dunmore's Proclamation offers freedom to enslaved people
events.description: On 7 November 1775, John Murray, Earl of Dunmore and Royal Governor of Virginia, issued a proclamation declaring martial law and offering freedom to enslaved people and indentured servants belonging to rebels who joined the British forces. An estimated 800–2,000 enslaved people reached British lines in response, forming the 'Ethiopian Regiment.' The proclamation outraged Virginia slaveholders, pushed many fence-sitters toward the patriot cause, and exposed the fundamental contradiction between revolutionary liberty rhetoric and the institution of slavery. It was one of the first significant emancipation offers of the war.
key_figures: John Murray, 4th Earl of Dunmore (Royal Governor of Virginia)

sources.name: Lord Dunmore's Proclamation, 7 November 1775 (Virginia Gazette, 23 November 1775)
Woody Holton, Forced Founders: Indians, Debtors, Slaves, and the Making of the American Revolution in Virginia (1999)

events.certainty_note: The proclamation text survives in multiple printed copies. Contemporary newspaper coverage is extensive.

events.title: Pugachev's Rebellion crushed; Pugachev executed
events.description: The massive Cossack and peasant revolt led by Yemelyan Pugachev, which had raged since 1773 across the Urals and Volga region, was decisively defeated in 1775. Pugachev was captured in September 1774 by his own followers, turned over to the authorities, and publicly executed in Moscow on 21 January 1775 by beheading and quartering. The rebellion — the largest popular uprising in Russian history before 1917 — had drawn Cossacks, Bashkirs, factory serfs, and peasants and briefly threatened Catherine II's control over vast territories. Its suppression led Catherine to restructure provincial administration through the Statute for the Administration of the Provinces of the Russian Empire (November 1775), strengthening central control.
key_figures: General Alexander Suvorov (helped suppress the rebellion)

sources.name: Alexander Pushkin, Istoriya Pugachyova (History of Pugachev, 1834) — based on archival research and interviews with survivors
John T. Alexander, Emperor of the Cossacks: Pugachev and the Frontier Jacquerie of 1773–1775 (1973)
Imperial Russian court records and interrogation transcripts of Pugachev (Russian State Archives)

events.certainty_note: Pugachev's trial and execution are documented in official Russian records. The date and circumstances are not in dispute.

events.title: Catherine II's Provincial Reform (Statute for the Governance of Provinces)
events.description: On 7 November 1775, Catherine II promulgated the Uchrezhdeniye o guberniyakh (Statute for the Administration of the Provinces), the most significant administrative reform of her reign. Directly motivated by the Pugachev Rebellion's exposure of weak provincial governance, the statute reorganised Russia from about 20 large provinces into approximately 50 smaller gubernii, each with standardised judicial, police, and fiscal institutions. The reform devolved certain functions to the provincial nobility, strengthening the gentry's local authority while extending the central state's administrative reach into previously ungovernable territories.
key_figures: Count Zakhar Chernyshyov (War College president, instrumental in implementation)

sources.name: Polnoye Sobraniye Zakonov Rossiyskoy Imperii (Complete Collection of Laws of the Russian Empire), No. 14,392
John P. LeDonne, Ruling Russia: Politics and Administration in the Age of Absolutism, 1762–1796 (1984)

events.certainty_note: The statute survives in full in the Russian legal corpus. Implementation is well-documented in provincial archives.

events.title: First Anglo-Maratha War continues; Treaty of Surat
events.description: The First Anglo-Maratha War (1775–1782) commenced in earnest in 1775 when the Bombay Presidency of the British East India Company intervened in a Maratha succession dispute by backing the pretender Raghunathrao against the Peshwa regency council. The Treaty of Surat (6 March 1775) between the Company and Raghunathrao promised British military support in exchange for territorial concessions including Salsette Island and Bassein. British forces seized Salsette in December 1774–early 1775. The intervention drew the Company into a protracted war against the formidable Maratha Confederacy that would expose serious limits to British military power in India.
key_figures: Nana Fadnavis (de facto leader of the Peshwa regency)

sources.name: Treaty of Surat, 6 March 1775 (East India Company records, British Library, India Office Records)
Bombay Presidency correspondence and council minutes, 1775 (India Office Records)
Randolf G.S. Cooper, The Anglo-Maratha Campaigns and the Contest for India (2003)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text and East India Company records survive. Maratha sources also confirm the political dynamics.

events.title: Qing suppression of Jinchuan rebellions completed
events.description: The Second Jinchuan Campaign (1771–1776), one of the Qianlong Emperor's 'Ten Great Campaigns,' continued through 1775 with brutal fighting against the Gyalrong Tibetan chieftains in the mountainous frontier of western Sichuan. The campaign was extraordinarily expensive — ultimately costing over 70 million taels of silver — and required massive troop commitments in difficult terrain. The war reflected Qing determination to impose direct control over semi-autonomous indigenous chieftains (tusi) on the empire's southwestern frontier, replacing them with centrally appointed officials in the gaitu guiliu ('replace native chiefs with regular bureaucrats') policy.

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing), Qianlong reign, 1775 entries
Joanna Waley-Cohen, The Culture of War in China: Empire and the Military under the Qing Dynasty (2006)
Dai Yingcong, The Sichuan Frontier and Tibet: Imperial Strategy in the Early Qing (2009)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Qing official records. Military expenditure figures are from imperial financial records.

events.title: James Watt's improved steam engine enters commercial production
events.description: In 1775, the partnership between James Watt and Matthew Boulton began commercial production of Watt's improved steam engine at the Soho Manufactory in Birmingham. Watt's crucial innovation — the separate condenser, patented in 1769 — dramatically improved fuel efficiency over the Newcomen engine. On 22 May 1775, Watt also secured an Act of Parliament extending his patent to 1800. The first engines were delivered to Bloomfield Colliery and John Wilkinson's ironworks. The Boulton & Watt engine would become the prime mover of the Industrial Revolution, transforming mining, manufacturing, and eventually transportation.
key_figures: John Wilkinson (ironmaster, early customer and collaborator who bored precise cylinders)

sources.name: Boulton & Watt business correspondence, 1775 (Birmingham Archives, Boulton & Watt Collection)
Steam Engine Act 1775 (15 Geo. III, c. 61) — Act of Parliament extending Watt's patent
Jenny Uglow, The Lunar Men: Five Friends Whose Curiosity Changed the World (2002)

events.certainty_note: Business records, the patent extension act, and early engine delivery records survive. The chronology is well-established.

events.title: Flour War (Guerre des Farines) in France
events.region: Paris and Île-de-France, Kingdom of France
events.description: In April–May 1775, a wave of bread riots swept through Paris, Versailles, and surrounding regions after Controller-General Turgot liberalised the grain trade by abolishing price controls. Grain prices spiked following a poor 1774 harvest, and crowds attacked grain convoys, bakeries, and markets. On 2–3 May, rioters reached Versailles and Paris. Louis XVI and Turgot suppressed the unrest with military force; two rioters were publicly hanged. The episode demonstrated the political danger of physiocratic free-market reforms in a society where bread accounted for the majority of working-class expenditure, and foreshadowed the subsistence crises that would fuel the French Revolution.
key_figures: Anne Robert Jacques Turgot (Controller-General of Finances)
Jean-Baptiste de Sartine (Lieutenant General of Police)

sources.name: Turgot's correspondence and edicts on the grain trade, 1774–1775 (Archives Nationales)
Cynthia Bouton, The Flour War: Gender, Class, and Community in Late Ancien Régime French Society (1993)
Steven Kaplan, Bread, Politics and Political Economy in the Reign of Louis XV (1976)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in police records, Turgot's correspondence, and contemporary accounts. The chronology and causes are well-established.

events.title: Pierre-Augustin Caron de Beaumarchais begins covert French aid to American rebels
events.description: In 1775, playwright and secret agent Beaumarchais began lobbying the French court to provide covert support to the American rebels as a means of weakening Britain. He submitted memoranda to Louis XVI arguing that an independent America would shatter British commercial dominance. By late 1775 and into 1776, these efforts led to the creation of the fictitious trading company Rodrigue Hortalez et Cie, through which France (and Spain) funnelled arms, ammunition, and supplies to the Continental Army. This covert aid was militarily significant well before France's formal alliance with America in 1778.
key_figures: Pierre-Augustin Caron de Beaumarchais (playwright, secret agent)
Charles Gravier, comte de Vergennes (French Foreign Minister)

sources.name: Beaumarchais' memoranda to Louis XVI, 1775–1776 (Archives du Ministère des Affaires Étrangères)
Jonathan Dull, A Diplomatic History of the American Revolution (1985)

events.certainty_note: Beaumarchais' memoranda survive in French diplomatic archives. The covert aid programme is well-documented.

events.title: Captain James Cook returns from second Pacific voyage
events.description: Captain James Cook returned to England on 30 July 1775 aboard HMS Resolution, completing his second voyage of exploration (1772–1775). The expedition had definitively disproved the existence of a habitable southern continent (Terra Australis) by sailing deep into Antarctic waters, crossed the Antarctic Circle three times, and charted numerous Pacific islands including New Caledonia, South Georgia, and the South Sandwich Islands. Cook also demonstrated that scurvy could be prevented through diet (especially sauerkraut, malt wort, and fresh provisions), losing only one man to disease during the three-year voyage — a revolutionary achievement in maritime health.

sources.name: James Cook, A Voyage towards the South Pole and Round the World (1777)
Resolution and Adventure ship logs, 1772–1775 (The National Archives, Kew)
J.C. Beaglehole, The Life of Captain James Cook (1974)

events.certainty_note: Ship logs and Cook's own published account provide precise dates and routes. The return date is not disputed.

events.title: Founding of the Khalsa Sikh Misl consolidation under Karor Singhia
events.description: By the mid-1770s, the twelve Sikh Misls (confederate warrior bands) had consolidated control over much of the Punjab following the collapse of effective Mughal authority and the decline of Afghan incursions. In 1775, the Misls were engaged in internal rivalries and territorial consolidation. The Bhangi, Kanhaiya, and Sukerchakia Misls were among the most powerful. This period laid the groundwork for Ranjit Singh's eventual unification of the Sikh Empire. The decentralised Misl system represented a distinctive form of political organisation combining martial culture with Khalsa religious identity.
key_figures: Jai Singh Kanhaiya (chief of the Kanhaiya Misl)
Jhanda Singh Bhangi (chief of the Bhangi Misl)
Maha Singh Sukerchakia (father of Ranjit Singh)

sources.name: Ratan Singh Bhangu, Prachin Panth Prakash (1841) — early Sikh chronicle drawing on oral traditions
J.S. Grewal, The Sikhs of the Punjab (Cambridge History of India series, 1990)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Misl politics in the 1770s is well-established, but precise dates for many events rely on later chronicles and oral tradition rather than contemporary documentation.

events.title: Ottoman-Russian tensions following Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca
events.description: In the aftermath of the Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca (1774), which ended the Russo-Turkish War and established Russian protectorate rights over Orthodox Christians in the Ottoman Empire, 1775 saw both empires manoeuvring over the newly 'independent' Crimean Khanate. Russia worked to install a client khan (Şahin Giray), while the Ottomans attempted to maintain influence. The treaty had been a humiliating defeat for the Porte, stripping it of direct sovereignty over Crimea and granting Russia navigation rights on the Black Sea. The unstable settlement set the stage for the Russian annexation of Crimea in 1783.

sources.name: Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca, 21 July 1774 (ratified 1775)
Virginia Aksan, Ottoman Wars 1700–1870: An Empire Besieged (2007)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives in both Russian and Ottoman archives. Diplomatic manoeuvring in 1775 is documented in both empires' records.

events.title: Habsburg acquisition of Bukovina from the Ottoman Empire
events.description: On 7 May 1775, the Ottoman Empire formally ceded the region of Bukovina to the Habsburg Empire through a convention agreed under pressure. The Habsburgs had occupied the territory — the northwestern part of the Principality of Moldavia — in 1774, ostensibly as compensation for mediating the Russo-Turkish peace and as a land corridor connecting Habsburg Transylvania to Galicia. The acquisition was achieved through diplomatic coercion of the weakened Porte. For Moldavia, the loss was deeply resented; the region's incorporation into the Habsburg system brought German-speaking administrators and significant demographic changes.
key_figures: Maria Theresa (Habsburg Empress, had moral reservations about the annexation)
Grigore III Ghica (Prince of Moldavia, protested the cession, later assassinated)

sources.name: Austro-Ottoman Convention on Bukovina, 7 May 1775 (Haus-, Hof- und Staatsarchiv, Vienna)
R.J.W. Evans, Austria, Hungary, and the Habsburgs: Central Europe c. 1683–1867 (2006)

events.certainty_note: The treaty text survives. Maria Theresa's famous private reservations ('She wept, but she took') are documented in contemporary court correspondence.

events.title: Tupac Amaru II begins organizing against Spanish colonial abuses
events.region: Viceroyalty of Peru (Upper Peru / modern Peru-Bolivia)
events.description: In the mid-1770s, José Gabriel Condorcanqui (Túpac Amaru II), a curaca (indigenous chief) and descendant of the last Inca ruler, was increasingly active in petitioning Spanish colonial authorities against the exploitative repartimiento (forced purchase) system, the mita (forced mining labour), and abuses by corregidores (provincial administrators). His legal petitions through official channels during 1775–1779 were repeatedly rejected, contributing to his radicalisation and eventual armed uprising in 1780 — the largest indigenous rebellion in colonial Latin American history.
key_figures: José Gabriel Condorcanqui / Túpac Amaru II (curaca of Tinta, future rebel leader)
José Antonio de Areche (Visitador General of Peru)

sources.name: Petitions and legal documents of Túpac Amaru II, 1770s (Archivo General de Indias, Seville)
Charles F. Walker, The Tupac Amaru Rebellion (2014)

events.certainty_note: Túpac Amaru's petitions survive in Spanish colonial archives. The exact timeline of his radicalisation in 1775 specifically is reconstructed; the broader pattern is well-established.

events.title: Elihu Palmer and the Great Dismal Swamp maroon communities
events.description: Throughout the 1770s, the Great Dismal Swamp on the Virginia-North Carolina border harboured significant communities of self-liberated enslaved people (maroons) who had established semi-permanent settlements in the swamp's interior. By 1775, with the upheaval of the Revolution and Lord Dunmore's Proclamation offering freedom to enslaved people who fled rebel owners, these communities grew. The maroon settlements of the Great Dismal Swamp represent one of the most significant examples of sustained enslaved resistance in mainland North America, though they are poorly documented because participants had every reason to avoid leaving records.

sources.name: Daniel O. Sayers, A Desolate Place for a Defiant People: The Archaeology of Maroons, Indigenous Americans, and Enslaved Laborers in the Great Dismal Swamp (2014)
Sylviane A. Diouf, Slavery's Exiles: The Story of the American Maroons (2014)

events.certainty_note: Archaeological evidence confirms the existence of maroon communities; the precise scale and dates are necessarily approximate due to the clandestine nature of these settlements.

events.title: Arrival of Franciscan missions in Alta California continues
events.region: Alta California, Viceroyalty of New Spain
events.description: The Spanish Franciscan mission system in Alta California, initiated in 1769, continued expanding in 1775 under the direction of Father Junípero Serra. Mission San Juan Capistrano was initially founded on 30 October 1775 (though abandoned within days due to news of the Kumeyaay revolt at San Diego and re-established in 1776). On 5 November 1775, the Kumeyaay people attacked and burned Mission San Diego de Alcalá, killing Father Luis Jayme — the first missionary killed in Alta California. The attack was a significant act of indigenous resistance against the coercive mission system, which forcibly relocated and confined indigenous people.
key_figures: Father Junípero Serra (President of the Alta California missions)
Father Luis Jayme (killed in the Kumeyaay attack)
Captain Fernando Rivera y Moncada (military commander of California)

sources.name: Father Serra's correspondence, 1775 (Santa Barbara Mission Archive-Library)
Steven Hackel, Junípero Serra: California's Founding Father (2013)
Richard Carrico, Strangers in a Stolen Land: Indians of San Diego County from Prehistory to the New Deal (2008)

events.certainty_note: Serra's letters and Spanish military reports document these events in detail. The Kumeyaay attack date is confirmed in multiple contemporary accounts.

events.title: Zand dynasty reaches peak influence under Karim Khan in Persia
events.description: Under Karim Khan Zand, who ruled as Vakil al-Ra'aya ('Advocate of the People') rather than Shah, Persia experienced a period of relative stability and cultural flourishing centred on the capital Shiraz. By 1775, Karim Khan had consolidated control over most of Persia (excluding Khorasan, held by the Afshars) and was presiding over a modest cultural renaissance. Shiraz was beautified with gardens, bazaars, and the Arg-e Karim Khan citadel. However, Karim Khan's health was declining, and his death in 1779 would plunge Persia into a succession crisis that paved the way for the Qajar dynasty.
key_figures: Karim Khan Zand (ruler of Persia, Vakil al-Ra'aya)

sources.name: John Perry, Karim Khan Zand: A History of Iran, 1747–1779 (1979)
European travellers' accounts from Shiraz, 1770s (various)

events.certainty_note: Karim Khan's reign is documented in both Persian chronicles and European travellers' accounts. The general characterisation of the period is well-established.

events.title: Foundation of the Asiatic Society planned; Hastings' cultural patronage
events.description: Warren Hastings, the first Governor-General of British India (appointed under the Regulating Act of 1773), pursued a policy of cultural engagement with Indian learning during the mid-1770s. In 1775, he commissioned Charles Wilkins to study Sanskrit and supported Nathaniel Brassey Halhed's work on A Code of Gentoo Laws (1776), one of the first English-language compilations of Hindu law. These efforts, while embedded in colonial power dynamics and oriented toward facilitating British governance, initiated systematic European engagement with Sanskrit literature and Indian intellectual traditions that would shape Orientalist scholarship.
key_figures: Charles Wilkins (East India Company official, Sanskrit scholar)
Nathaniel Brassey Halhed (scholar, compiler of Hindu law)

sources.name: Warren Hastings' correspondence, 1775 (British Library, India Office Records)
Thomas R. Trautmann, Aryans and British India (1997)

events.certainty_note: Hastings' patronage of Indological scholarship is documented in his own correspondence and Company records.

events.title: Antoine Lavoisier's memoir on the nature of combustion
events.description: In 1775, Antoine Lavoisier presented his landmark memoir 'Mémoire sur la nature du principe qui se combine avec les métaux pendant leur calcination' to the French Academy of Sciences, arguing that combustion and calcination involved combination with a component of air (which he later named 'oxygen') rather than the release of phlogiston. Building on his sealed-vessel experiments, Lavoisier demonstrated that metals gained weight during calcination precisely equal to the weight of air absorbed. This work was a critical step in dismantling the phlogiston theory and establishing the oxygen theory of combustion, one of the foundational achievements of modern chemistry.
key_figures: Joseph Priestley (whose isolation of 'dephlogisticated air' in 1774 informed Lavoisier's work)

sources.name: Antoine Lavoisier, 'Mémoire sur la nature du principe qui se combine avec les métaux pendant leur calcination,' Mémoires de l'Académie Royale des Sciences (1775)
Frederic Lawrence Holmes, Antoine Lavoisier — The Next Crucial Year (1998)

events.certainty_note: The memoir survives in the Academy's records. Lavoisier's laboratory notebooks corroborate the experiments described.

events.title: Partition of the Duchy of Courland debated
events.description: The Duchy of Courland, a vassal state of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, continued to be a site of Russian influence and internal political conflict in 1775. Duke Peter von Biron, installed with Russian backing, ruled a duchy increasingly subject to the competing pressures of Russian expansionism and the weakened Polish-Lithuanian state. The situation reflected the broader pattern of great-power manipulation of smaller polities in Eastern Europe that had produced the First Partition of Poland in 1772 and would lead to Courland's final absorption by Russia in 1795.

sources.name: Andrejs Plakans, The Latvians: A Short History (1995)

events.certainty_note: The general political dynamics are well-established, though the specific events of 1775 in Courland are less richly documented than the major power politics.

disconfirming_evidence: Several commonly repeated claims about 1775 merit qualification. (1) The 'shot heard round the world' at Lexington: who fired first on 19 April remains genuinely unknown. Both sides produced depositions blaming the other; modern scholarship (Fischer 1994) concludes the question is unresolvable. The phrase itself comes from Emerson's 1837 poem, not from 1775. (2) The Battle of Bunker Hill is often presented as a near-victory for the colonials, but recent scholarship emphasises that the British achieved their tactical objective (taking the heights) and the colonial retreat was disorderly. The battle's significance lay in the casualties inflicted, not in any colonial tactical success. (3) Lavoisier's 1775 memoir is sometimes presented as the moment he 'discovered oxygen,' but this overstates the case — Joseph Priestley had isolated the gas in 1774, and Carl Wilhelm Scheele may have done so even earlier (c. 1771–1772, published 1777). Lavoisier's contribution was theoretical reinterpretation, not discovery of the substance itself. (4) The Flour War is sometimes cited as a direct precursor to the French Revolution, but many historians (notably Kaplan) caution against teleological readings — subsistence riots were common in ancien régime France, and the 1775 events were suppressed without lasting institutional change. (5) Cook's anti-scurvy measures are sometimes credited to citrus fruits, but Cook himself was sceptical of citrus and credited malt wort and sauerkraut; the link between citrus and scurvy prevention was not definitively established until the 1790s.
historiographic_note: 1775 is richly documented, but the surviving record is heavily weighted toward the perspectives of literate, elite, and European actors. The American Revolution dominates Anglophone historiography, potentially distorting the relative global significance of events. British colonial records provide detailed documentation of India and North America but through an imperial lens. Qing records are extensive but court-centered. The experiences of enslaved people, indigenous peoples, and non-elite populations are largely reconstructed from archaeological evidence, legal records (petitions, court cases), and the observations of their oppressors rather than their own voices. The Flour War participants, Kumeyaay resisters, and Great Dismal Swamp maroons left few or no written records of their own perspectives.

graph_edges.note: Lexington and Concord opened armed conflict that escalated to the Declaration of Independence
The Second Continental Congress became the body that declared independence in July 1776
Catherine's provincial reform was a direct response to the failures exposed by Pugachev's Rebellion
Boulton & Watt engines became the key prime mover of industrial manufacturing
Subsistence crisis and failed reform foreshadow the revolutionary crisis of 1789, though the connection is not direct causation
Beaumarchais' covert aid programme preceded and facilitated formal French alliance with America
Post-Küçük Kaynarca manoeuvring over Crimea led directly to Russian annexation in 1783
Failed legal petitions in the 1770s radicalised Túpac Amaru II toward armed rebellion
The 1775 memoir was a key step toward Lavoisier's systematic chemical revolution published in 1789
Dunmore's Proclamation likely increased flight to maroon communities like those in the Great Dismal Swamp
The Treaty of Surat initiated the First Anglo-Maratha War that concluded with the Treaty of Salbai in 1782

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
